name: GuardDuty Findings and Threat Detection
description: Intelligent threat detection service that continuously monitors AWS accounts and workloads for malicious activity
key_facts: Analyzes CloudTrail events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS logs automatically
Findings categorized by severity: Low, Medium, High
Supports multi-account management via AWS Organizations delegated administrator
Malware Protection scans EBS volumes attached to EC2 instances
S3 Protection monitors object-level API operations for anomalies
common_misconceptions: GuardDuty blocks threats automatically (it only detects and alerts, remediation requires separate action)
GuardDuty requires enabling CloudTrail separately (it reads CloudTrail management events independently)

name: AWS Security Hub
description: Central security dashboard that aggregates findings from multiple AWS security services and third-party tools
key_facts: Aggregates findings in AWS Security Finding Format (ASFF)
Automated compliance checks against CIS AWS Foundations, PCI DSS, and AWS Foundational Security Best Practices
Cross-region and cross-account aggregation via delegated administrator
Custom actions trigger EventBridge rules for automated remediation
Integrates with GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, Firewall Manager, and third-party products
common_misconceptions: Security Hub replaces individual security services (it aggregates their findings but does not replace them)
Security Hub automatically remediates findings (it reports findings; remediation requires custom automation)

name: Amazon Detective
description: Security service that uses graph analysis to investigate and identify root causes of security findings
key_facts: Automatically builds a behavior graph from CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, and GuardDuty findings
Retains up to 12 months of aggregated data for investigation
Visualizes resource interactions and API call patterns over time
Integrates directly with GuardDuty findings for one-click investigation
common_misconceptions: Detective detects threats (it investigates findings from GuardDuty and other sources, it does not detect them)
Detective replaces GuardDuty (Detective is for post-detection investigation, GuardDuty is for detection)

name: CloudTrail for Security Auditing
description: Governance, compliance, and audit service that records AWS API calls for security analysis
key_facts: Management events enabled by default; data events require explicit configuration
CloudTrail Lake provides SQL-based querying of events for up to 7 years
Organization trail captures events across all accounts in an AWS Organization
Log file integrity validation detects tampering using SHA-256 hashing
Insights events detect unusual API activity patterns
common_misconceptions: CloudTrail logs data events by default (only management events are logged by default)
CloudTrail events appear instantly (there can be up to 15-minute delay for delivery to S3)

name: VPC Flow Logs and S3 Access Logging
description: Network traffic logging for VPCs and access logging for S3 buckets to support security monitoring
key_facts: VPC Flow Logs capture IP traffic at VPC, subnet, or ENI level
Flow logs can be sent to CloudWatch Logs, S3, or Kinesis Data Firehose
Custom format allows selecting specific fields to reduce costs
S3 server access logging records requests made to an S3 bucket in a target bucket
Flow logs do not capture DNS traffic to Route 53 Resolver, DHCP, or instance metadata traffic
common_misconceptions: VPC Flow Logs capture packet contents (they only capture metadata like source, destination, ports, and action)
S3 access logs are guaranteed to be complete (they are delivered on a best-effort basis)

name: AWS Config for Compliance Monitoring
description: Service that tracks resource configurations and evaluates them against desired settings for compliance
key_facts: Records resource configuration changes and maintains a configuration history
Managed rules cover common compliance checks like encrypted EBS, public S3, and required tags
Custom rules use Lambda functions for organization-specific evaluations
Conformance packs bundle multiple Config rules and remediation actions as a single entity
Automatic remediation via SSM Automation documents when resources become non-compliant
common_misconceptions: Config prevents non-compliant resources from being created (it evaluates after creation; use SCPs for prevention)
Config rules evaluate in real-time (evaluation triggers on configuration changes or periodic schedules)

name: WAF Rules and Shield Advanced
description: Web application firewall and DDoS protection services for protecting AWS resources
key_facts: WAF attaches to CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, and AppSync
Rule groups are reusable collections of rules with capacity units (WCU) limit of 5000 per web ACL
Managed rule groups from AWS and AWS Marketplace provide pre-built protections
Shield Standard is free and protects against common Layer 3/4 DDoS attacks
Shield Advanced provides cost protection, DDoS Response Team access, and advanced metrics
common_misconceptions: WAF can be attached to EC2 instances directly (it requires CloudFront, ALB, API Gateway, or AppSync)
Shield Standard requires subscription (it is automatically included at no extra cost)

name: AWS Network Firewall
description: Managed network firewall service for fine-grained control over VPC network traffic
key_facts: Stateful and stateless rule groups for Layer 3-7 traffic inspection
Supports Suricata-compatible IPS/IDS rules for deep packet inspection
Deployed in a dedicated firewall subnet in each AZ
Integrates with Firewall Manager for centralized policy across multiple accounts
Supports TLS inspection for encrypted traffic analysis
common_misconceptions: Network Firewall replaces security groups and NACLs (it provides complementary Layer 7 inspection)
Network Firewall is the same as WAF (WAF is Layer 7 HTTP/HTTPS only; Network Firewall covers Layers 3-7)

name: Security Groups Best Practices
description: Designing least-privilege security group configurations for defense-in-depth network security
key_facts: Reference other security groups instead of CIDR blocks for dynamic environments
Use separate security groups per tier (web, app, database) for layered security
Prefix lists simplify management of common CIDR ranges across security groups
VPC security group rules support descriptions for audit documentation
common_misconceptions: Opening port 0-65535 to a security group is safe because it is internal (it violates least-privilege even internally)
Security groups alone provide sufficient network security (defense-in-depth requires NACLs and firewall layers)

name: Cross-Account Roles and Permission Boundaries
description: Configuring secure cross-account access patterns and limiting maximum permissions with boundaries
key_facts: Cross-account roles require a trust policy in the target account and sts:AssumeRole in the source account
Permission boundaries set the maximum permissions an IAM entity can have regardless of identity policies
Effective permissions are the intersection of identity-based policies and permission boundaries
External ID in trust policies prevents confused deputy attacks from third-party access
Session policies further restrict permissions when assuming a role via STS
common_misconceptions: Permission boundaries grant permissions (they only limit the maximum allowed permissions)
External ID is a secret authentication mechanism (it is a public identifier to prevent cross-account confusion)

name: ABAC and Identity Federation
description: Attribute-based access control and federated identity patterns for scalable access management
key_facts: ABAC uses resource and principal tags in IAM policy conditions for scalable access control
SAML 2.0 federation maps identity provider attributes to IAM role session tags
IAM Identity Center (SSO) provides centralized workforce access to multiple AWS accounts
Web identity federation with Cognito supports mobile and web application authentication
ABAC eliminates the need to update policies when new resources are created if tags are consistent
common_misconceptions: ABAC replaces RBAC entirely (they are complementary; ABAC works best alongside role-based controls)
Cognito User Pools and Identity Pools are the same (User Pools handle authentication, Identity Pools handle authorization to AWS services)

name: KMS Key Policies and CMK Rotation
description: Managing encryption keys with KMS key policies, grants, and automatic key rotation
key_facts: Key policy is the primary access control mechanism for KMS keys and is required
Key policy must explicitly allow IAM policies to control access (via the root account statement)
Automatic key rotation rotates backing key material annually while keeping the same key ID
Imported key material does not support automatic rotation; manual rotation creates a new key
Grants provide temporary, granular access to KMS keys without modifying key policies
common_misconceptions: IAM policies alone can control KMS access (the key policy must allow IAM policy evaluation first)
Key rotation changes the key ID (the key ID stays the same; only the backing material changes)

name: S3 Encryption and ACM
description: Encryption options for S3 objects and TLS certificate management with AWS Certificate Manager
key_facts: SSE-S3 uses AES-256 with Amazon-managed keys and is the default encryption for new buckets
SSE-KMS provides audit trail via CloudTrail and allows key policy control over access
SSE-C requires the customer to manage and supply keys with every request
S3 Bucket Keys reduce KMS API costs by generating a bucket-level key for SSE-KMS
ACM provides free public TLS certificates that auto-renew for use with CloudFront, ALB, and API Gateway
common_misconceptions: SSE-S3 provides per-object key access control (only SSE-KMS provides that via key policies)
ACM certificates can be exported for use on EC2 (only ACM Private CA certificates can be exported)

name: Secrets Manager and Parameter Store
description: Securely storing and automatically rotating secrets, credentials, and configuration data
key_facts: Secrets Manager supports automatic rotation using Lambda functions for RDS, Redshift, and DocumentDB
Cross-account secret sharing via resource-based policies
Parameter Store Standard tier is free; Advanced tier supports larger values and policies
Secrets Manager encrypts secrets with KMS and charges per secret per month
Parameter Store SecureString parameters are encrypted with KMS but lack native rotation
common_misconceptions: Parameter Store and Secrets Manager are interchangeable (Secrets Manager adds automatic rotation and cross-account sharing)
Secrets Manager rotation happens without any Lambda function (a Lambda function performs the rotation logic)

name: SCPs, Config Rules, and Audit Manager
description: Preventive and detective governance controls across AWS Organizations accounts
key_facts: SCPs are preventive controls that define maximum permissions for organization member accounts
SCPs do not affect the management account or service-linked roles
Conformance packs deploy a collection of Config rules and remediation actions as a single unit
AWS Audit Manager automates evidence collection for compliance frameworks like SOC 2 and GDPR
Tag policies enforce standardized tagging across organization accounts
common_misconceptions: SCPs affect the management account (they do not; the management account is always unrestricted by SCPs)
Conformance packs automatically remediate issues (remediation actions must be explicitly configured)
